coredata.dc:title: A novel dual-color ratiometric fluorescent probe for rapid and sensitive monitoring of oxalate in urolithiasis urine
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Weng, Qin
Cao, Lidong
Nikolaevich, Artyom
Dong, Mengmeng
Sheng, Qiuping
coredata.dc:description: The concentration of oxalate in human urine is a key disease biomarker for the assessment of the formation and prognosis of clinical urolithiasis. Compared with other detection methods, ratiometric fluorescent probes have the advantages of high precision, non-invasiveness, visualization, rapidity, convenience, and low cost. However, constructing ratiometric fluorescent sensors for real-time detection of oxalate in urine samples remains challenging. Here, we successfully developed a dual-color ratiometric fluorescent probe for rapid and sensitive detection of oxalate based on the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) effect between dual-fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CDs) and Si materials. In the initial state, the blue fluorescence of CDs confined in Si channels is effectively quenched by the red fluorescence of Si due to the FRET effect. However, the fluorescent Si particles serve as a highly sensitive sensing platform for copper ions, and their red fluorescence can be efficiently and irreversibly quenched by trace amounts of copper ions. As a result, the FRET effect is weakened, and the CDs emit blue fluorescence. With the increase of oxalate concentration, the red/blue ratio (R/B) of the system continuously decreases. Under optimized conditions, the ratiometric probe exhibits a wide working range for oxalate detection, with a low limit of detection (LOD) of 0.09 μM and good selectivity for oxalate. Our proposed method shows consistent results with commercial detection kits in real urine samples, indicating its great potential for application in the metabolic assessment of urolithiasis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Urolithiasis
Oxalate detection
Ratiometric fluorescence
Urine biosensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Choice, preparation and characterization of Si probe and CDs
Cu2+ induced fluorescent color change of C-Si probe

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Fluorescenceimage of Si particles at excitation wavelength of 360 nm. (B) SEM images of the mesoporous structure of Si particles. (C)TEMimage and the corresponding particle-size distribution of theCDs. (D) The fluorescence spectraof CDs at excitation wavelength of 360 nm and UV–visabsorption spectrumof Si particles.

(A) Schematic of the mechanism of Cu2+ induced fluorescent color change of C-Si probe. (B) Fluorescenceimage of C-Si particles at excitation wavelength of 360 nm. (C) Fluorescencespectra images of C-Si particles at excitation wavelength of 360 nm. (D)The relationship between R/B value and Cu2+ concentration. (E)The relationship between R/B value and time. (F) EDS results of C-Si particles after Cu2+ quenching.
Optimization on oxalate concentration (A), particle content (B), reaction time (C) and pH value (D) of the proposed method.

Detection of oxalate in aqueous solution. Fluorescence images (A) and spectra (B) of C-Si probe after adding various concentrations of oxalate. (C) Fluorescence spectra of C-Si probe after adding 0, 0.1 and 1 μM oxalate. (D) Working curve for oxalate. (E) The specificity for oxalate analysis towards common substances in urine.
Detection of oxalate in 6 urine samples including 3 normal people without urolithiasis and 3 urolithiasis patients by ELISA and proposed method.

head.ce:title: A novel dual-color ratiometric fluorescent probe for rapid and sensitive monitoring of oxalate in urolithiasis urine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Lomonosov Moscow State University, Moscow, Russia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Lomonosov Moscow State University, Moscow, Russia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The concentration of oxalate in human urine is a key disease biomarker for the assessment of the formation and prognosis of clinical urolithiasis. Compared with other detection methods, ratiometric fluorescent probes have the advantages of high precision, non-invasiveness, visualization, rapidity, convenience, and low cost. However, constructing ratiometric fluorescent sensors for real-time detection of oxalate in urine samples remains challenging. Here, we successfully developed a dual-color ratiometric fluorescent probe for rapid and sensitive detection of oxalate based on the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) effect between dual-fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CDs) and Si materials. In the initial state, the blue fluorescence of CDs confined in Si channels is effectively quenched by the red fluorescence of Si due to the FRET effect. However, the fluorescent Si particles serve as a highly sensitive sensing platform for copper ions, and their red fluorescence can be efficiently and irreversibly quenched by trace amounts of copper ions. As a result, the FRET effect is weakened, and the CDs emit blue fluorescence. With the increase of oxalate concentration, the red/blue ratio (R/B) of the system continuously decreases. Under optimized conditions, the ratiometric probe exhibits a wide working range for oxalate detection, with a low limit of detection (LOD) of 0.09 μM and good selectivity for oxalate. Our proposed method shows consistent results with commercial detection kits in real urine samples, indicating its great potential for application in the metabolic assessment of urolithiasis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Urolithiasis is a common urinary system disease that refers to the precipitation and deposition of crystalline substances (such as calcium, oxalate, urate, etc.) from urine in parts of the kidneys [1,2]. In recent years, the incidence of urolithiasis has been on the rise, with an estimated global prevalence of 10–15 % [3]. Most urolithiasis do not require surgical treatment and can be managed through medical prevention and therapy [4]. However, the recurrence rate of urolithiasis is also high, with approximately 50 % of patients experiencing a relapse within 5 years [5,6]. Calcium oxalate stones are the most common type of urolithiasis, and they form in acidic or neutral urine, predominantly affecting males [7,8]. Oxalate (C2O4 2−) is a product of renal excretion of protein metabolism, and the level of oxalate in urine is usually tens to hundreds of times higher than that in blood [9,10]. There is ample evidence that excessive concentration of oxalate excreted in urine is a significant indicator for the formation of calcium oxalate stones [11]. Generally, when the concentration of oxalate in urine exceeds 460 μM, it can be considered that the patient is at a higher risk of urolithiasis [12]. Given the increasing incidence of oxalate stones in clinical practice, there is an urgent need to develop convenient and effective methods for oxalate determination.

In clinical practice, computed tomography (CT), ultrasonography, and infrared spectroscopy are commonly used for the rapid diagnosis of oxalate stones [13]. However, CT and ultrasonography cannot differentiate oxalate stones from other types of stones, such as phosphates, and the instruments are expensive. Infrared spectroscopy requires additional surgical lithotripsy and stone extraction steps, which are time-consuming and are generally used for qualitative analysis [14]. Therefore, early screening of patients with oxalate stones is becoming increasingly important. To date, various analytical strategies for oxalate sensing have been developed in recent years, including high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), electrochemical methods, ion chromatography, and gas chromatography [15–18]. Although these methods offer high precision, they always rely on bulky, expensive instruments and specialized operators, often suffering from low analytical throughput or cumbersome procedures. Among them, optical sensing approaches stand out due to their advantages, such as high detection sensitivity, rapid analysis, and ease of implementation for on-site and miniaturized point-of-care testing (POCT) [19–22]. For optical sensing, colorimetric and fluorescent strategies are expected to enable visual monitoring of oxalate content in POCT with the naked eye, which will be of great significance in practical applications [23,24]. For example, Tavallali et al. found that the combination of copper ions with an anthraquinone dye called Reactive Blue 4 (RB4) leads to significant color and UV–vis spectral changes [25]. Due to the high affinity of oxalate for copper ions, in the presence of oxalate, copper ions tend to bind with oxalate rather than the dye, resulting in further color changes that can be used for sensitive colorimetric analysis of oxalate. Recently, Chen et al. developed a series of fluorescent nanoprobes for the POCT analysis of oxalate [12,26,27]. In one of their works, they synthesized nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CD) and MnO2 nanosheets as a composite probe system to achieve highly sensitive detection of oxalate [27]. The detection principle is based on the ability of oxalate to reduce MnO2 to Mn2+, thereby weakening the ability of MnO2 to quench the fluorescence of N-CD through the inner filter effect (IFE). The work indicates that the strong reducing or chelating ability of oxalate for metal ions can effectively modulate the quenching ability of metal ions for colorimetric/fluorescent probes, thereby enabling the construction of highly sensitive and selective visual sensors.

Despite the progress in colorimetric/fluorescent oxalate detection methods, the visual differences of a single-color solution at low analyte concentrations are not significant and are difficult to identify with the naked eye. Ratiometric fluorescent probes provide self-calibration references by measuring fluorescence signals at different wavelengths and calculating intensity ratios, reducing environmental effects and enabling sensitive detection [28]. Unlike traditional color brightness change detection, the ratiometric method detects target analytes by generating different colors with characteristic hues, thereby significantly improving the sensitivity of visual quantitative measurements [29]. Since the human eye is more sensitive to hue changes than to brightness changes, this method can also significantly reduce visual fatigue [30,31]. To further improve sensitivity, nanomaterials with unique optical and physicochemical properties have been introduced into the design of ratiometric fluorescent probes. Over the past few decades, ratiometric fluorescent systems based on nanomaterials have been widely reported and successfully applied to the detection and imaging of various substances [32–34]. For instance, Cui et al. developed a ratiometric fluorescent detection system based on fluorescent Si particles and quantum dots [35]. In this system, thanks to the quantum confinement effect and the introduction of surface oxidation energy levels, the activated luminescent Si particles exhibit red fluorescence (emission peak: 650–750 nm). After encapsulating the blue fluorescent CDs in the pore structure of Si, the fluorescence of CDs in the nanocomposite is quenched due to the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) effect, and the probe exhibits red fluorescence. H2O2 can trigger the oxidation of Si and weaken the FRET effect, promoting the shift of the system from red to blue. This ratiometric probe system, which changes from red to blue fluorescence due to the concentration of the analyte, is very suitable for biochemical visualization analysis. However, to our knowledge, this Si-carbon composite nanoprobe has not yet been applied to the point-of-care analysis of inorganic ions, including oxalate.

Inspired by the above-mentioned studies, in this paper, we successfully synthesized fluorescent Si and CDs with luminescent properties and successfully constructed a C-Si hybrid nanocomposite by encapsulating CDs in the abundant pores of Si. Due to the presence of the FRET effect, the original emission peak of CDs at 400 nm is effectively quenched, and the probe exhibits bright red fluorescence. We found that copper ions cause irreversible quenching of Si fluorescence, which is attributed to Cu-induced irreversible oxidation, but has almost no effect on the blue fluorescence of CDs. Therefore, CDs can serve as an effective reference for ratiometric fluorescent probes. When oxalate is added, the high binding affinity between copper ions and oxalate significantly reduces the quenching effect of copper ions on Si fluorescence, and the red/blue (R/B) value of the C-Si system is proportional to the concentration of oxalate. These color and spectral changes can be captured by a fluorescence microscope and spectrometer connected to a CCD camera. Under optimized conditions, the detection limit of the probe for oxalate is as low as 0.9 μM. Most importantly, this system has been successfully applied to the determination of oxalate in clinical urine samples, which is comparable to the results obtained by commercial kits. Thanks to the low cost, visualization, rapidity, and sensitivity of the proposed method, it is expected that the C-Si probe will find widespread application in the point-of-care analysis of patients with oxalate stones.

Mesoporous Si was fabricated from single-side polished, boron-doped p-type Si wafers (0.0009 Ω cm resistivity, <100> orientation, sourced from Sil'tronix Si Technologies, Archamps, France), following established protocols [36]. In detail, anodization of the single-crystal Si wafer was conducted in a bespoke Teflon etching cell, utilizing a hydrofluoric acid and anhydrous ethanol mixture (ratio 7:2, v/v) at a constant current density of 100 mA/cm2, with a platinum ring acting as the counter electrode. This process yielded pores larger than 10 nm, suitable for quantum dot accommodation. Following anodization, the Si surface was cleaned with ethanol and dried under a nitrogen stream. Subsequently, the Si film was released from the wafer by applying a 4 mA/cm2 current pulse in a 3.3 % HF ethanol solution. The detached film was then soaked in a sodium borate buffer (6 mg/mL, pH 9.2) for 5 min to induce visible near-infrared luminescence, after which it was washed extensively with deionized water. The luminescent film was fragmented into microparticles by ultrasonication in deionized water for 5 h and then filtered through a 5 μm Millipore membrane to remove smaller particles. Blue-emitting CDs were synthesized via a high-temperature pyrolysis approach using glutamic acid, as previously detailed [37]. These CDs (20 mg/mL) were incorporated into the Si particles (10 mg/mL) through mechanical agitation at room temperature for 4 h. After three cycles of centrifugation and washing, the resultant composite particles were stored at 4 °C for subsequent applications.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were captured using a JEOL JSM-7600F scanning electron microscope. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the CDs were obtained using a JEOL JEM-2100 UHR transmission electron microscope. UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded with a Shimadzu UV-2450 spectrophotometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on a Thermo ScientificTM K-AlphaTM + spectrometer equipped with a monochromatic Al Kα X-ray source (1486.6 eV) operating at 100 W. The surface potential of the composite materials was measured using a Zeta potential analyzer (Nano ZSP, Malvern, England). Fluorescence (FL) images were acquired using a fluorescence microscope (Eclipse LV150, Nikon) equipped with a thermoelectrically cooled CCD camera (CoolSNAP HQ2, Photometrics), with an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. FL spectra were recorded using a fiber spectrometer (QE Pro). FL lifetime of CDs in aqueous solution was detected by Edinburgh Instruments FLS920 fluorescence spectrometer.

The sensitivity of the composite probe to Cu2+ was evaluated using CuCl2. A stock solution of CuCl2 (100 mM) was freshly prepared and serially diluted to obtain various concentrations. A 0.1 M NaAc-HAc buffer (pH = 7.0) was also used. To assess the quenching ability of Cu2+ on the composite probe, different concentrations of Cu2+ ion solutions were added to 2 mL of C-Si (1–50 mg/mL) buffer solution, with reagent blanks also performed. The fluorescence images and spectra of the C-Si particles were collected under 360 nm excitation, and the spectra were normalized based on the fluorescence of CDs at 400 nm. The system's FRET efficiency was calculated according to previous work [38].

The general procedure for oxalate analysis is as follows: First, 10 μL of CuCl2 (10 mM) was added to 400 μL of 0.1 M NaAc-HAc buffer (pH = 7) at room temperature. Then, 40 μL of oxalate solution at various concentrations was added to the mixture and reacted for 2 min. Subsequently, 50 μL of the probe solution (1 mg/mL) was added and further incubated at room temperature for 4 min. The fluorescence spectra of the C-Si probe were then measured, and the R/B values were recorded.

Clinical urine samples were provided by the People's Hospital of Zhejiang Province. This study was approved by the Biomedical Ethics Committee and consent was obtained from the patients. The entire detection procedure was completed within 24 h. The fresh urine samples were spiked with standard oxalate solutions at three concentrations (25, 50, 100 μM) for recovery tests. To detect the concentration of oxalate, the actual samples were preprocessed according to previously reported methods [12]. Specifically, the collected urine samples were centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 5 min at 4 ℃. Then, the supernatant was further ultrafiltered using a 3 kDa ultrafiltration centrifuge tube at 10,000 rpm at 4 ℃. Finally, to minimize matrix effects on detection accuracy, the solution in the outer tube was collected and diluted with ultrapure water before use [39,40]. The ELISA kit determines oxalate concentration by a coupled enzymatic reaction that produces a colorimetric product proportional to oxalate concentration at 450 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Choice, preparation and characterization of Si probe and CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In fact, the ratiometric fluorescence system composed of fluorescent CDs and porous Si has been reported in previous literature [35]. Compared with other materials, C-Si dual-color fluorescence materials have the following advantages. On one hand, fluorescent silicon (Si) material prepared from electrochemical etching has a wide range of advantages, including high loading capacity derived from its abundant porous structure, ease of preparation, low cost, tunable fluorescence, high degradability and good biocompatibility [41]. Due to its high density of electronic states and the large surface area of its porous structure, Si can be efficiently quenched by metal ions, ensuring the sensitivity of fluorescence analysis. On the other hand, as a member of the nanocarbon family, CDs exhibit the advantages of easy synthesis, high brightness, and good stability and biocompatibility [42]. Its small size can be well confined in PSi, and its own fluorescence is protected by the pore channels. Therefore, it can serve as a reference for ratiometric fluorescence. Based on these features, the nano-hybrid structure of C-PSi has great potential in bioassays [35]. For example, previous work has shown that the abundant porous structure of PSi is very conducive to loading small-sized CDs [43]. Interestingly, the FRET effect between blue-fluorescent CDs and red-fluorescent Si can effectively construct a stimulus-responsive fluorescence sensing system [35]. Moreover, the naked eye can easily distinguish the fluorescence color change from red to blue, indicating that this composite material has great potential as a multifunctional platform for ratiometric visual monitoring.

In this study, we initially prepared luminescent Si particles with abundant mesoporous structures using the classic electrochemical etching of Si wafers [44]. Then, the fluorescence of Si was facilitated by mild oxidation in a borate aqueous solution, due to the quantum confinement effect with the gradual reduction in Si crystal size [45]. The fluorescence microscopy image shown in Fig. 1
A distinctly illustrates the luminescent properties of the prepared Si particles with the emission of bright red fluorescence ranging from 600 to 900 nm. Additionally, SEM characterization further confirmed the abundant mesoporous structure (10–20 nm) of individual Si particles, which is suitable for quantum dot loading (Fig. 1 B). Further, TEM analysis was conducted on the prepared CDs. As depicted in Fig. 1 C, the CDs exhibit uniform distribution and good dispersion. Subsequently, by physical mixing and mechanical shaking, small-sized CDs can be effectively confined within the mesoporous structure of Si to fabricate the C-Si composite. Fig. 1 D presents the absorption spectrum of Si and the emission spectrum of CDs (excitation wavelength: 360 nm). The overlap between the two spectra indicates the potential for FRET between these two fluorescent materials. According to previous studies, red-fluorescent Si can act as an energy donor, while blue-fluorescent CDs serve as an effective energy acceptor [35]. Therefore, upon combination, the composite exhibits red fluorescence, with the fluorescence of the donor CDs being quenched. However, the quenching mechanism may also be derived from inner filter effect (IFE) effect. As shown in Fig. S1, serial dilution of Si did not recover fluorescence of CDs, excluding IFE as a significant contributor [46]. Furthermore, lifetime of free CDs (5.2 ns) and C-Si composite (3.5 ns) indicated that CD could strongly decrease the FL lifetime after incorporation with Si (Fig. S2). The shortened lifetime confirms non-radiative energy transfer (FRET), whereas IFE would not alter lifetime [47]. Notably, the ratio between CDs and Si particles could potentially affect FRET efficiency. As indicated in Fig. S3, the results showed remarkably consistent FRET efficiency (∼80 %) across this entire concentration range of CD stock solution (1–50 mg/mL), demonstrating that the porous Si matrix provides an excellent confined binding environment for efficient CD fluorescence quenching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cu2+ induced fluorescent color change of C-Si probe
ce:section.ce:para:
Subsequently, we employed this FRET system for fluorescence sensing. As previously mentioned, the high binding affinity of metal ions for oxalate can effectively facilitate the construction of novel sensing strategies. It is well-known that the fluorescence of Si particles can be efficiently quenched by metal ions [48,49]. Xia et al. reported that the quenching of Si fluorescence is highly sensitive to Cu ions. Even micromolar levels of copper ions present in cells can quench the red fluorescence of Si nanocrystals [50]. This highly sensitive and specific quenching phenomenon prompted us to investigate the sensitivity of the composite C-Si probe towards Cu ions, and the influence on FRET. As shown in Fig. 2
A, after loading with CDs, the blue fluorescence of CDs is quenched due to the FRET effect, and the composite probe exhibits bright red fluorescence originating from Si. Upon effective quenching of Si by copper ions, the blue fluorescence of CDs becomes visible. The key to this process is that the fluorescence of CDs, serving as an internal reference, should not be affected by Cu2+. To this end, we investigated the effect of different concentrations of CuCl2 on the fluorescence of CDs. As shown in Fig. S4, Cu2+ up to 1 mM have negligible effects on the blue fluorescence of CDs, whereas such concentrations are sufficient to cause irreversible quenching of Si fluorescence. Contrary to reports where Cu2+ quenches CD fluorescence [51,52], our glutamic acid-derived CDs exhibit Cu2+ resistance similar to CDs prepared from other precursors (e.g., citric acid) [53,54]. This behavior may originate from (i) carboxyl/amide passivation, (ii) pore confinement in Si, and (iii) preferential Cu2+ reduction by Si. Therefore, CDs can effectively serve as a reference for the ratiometric fluorescent sensor.

Next, we explored the influence of Cu2+ quenching on the FRET phenomenon. As shown in Fig. 2 B and C, with increasing Cu2+ concentration (0–200 μM), the fluorescence probe exhibited regular changes in both imaging and spectra. By recording the changes in the R/B ratio, we observed that the red fluorescence of Si almost disappeared when the Cu2+ concentration reached 200 μM (Fig. 2 D). More importantly, we found that this change could reach equilibrium within 1 min, indicating the high quenching efficiency of copper ions (Fig. 2 E) The mechanism of their fluorescence quenching of Si particles prepared by electrochemical etching of Si wafer by Cu2+ has been reported in detail from many literatures [50,55,56]. It had been proved that Cu2+ could dramatically reduce the quantum efficiency of porous Si, due to the capture of excited carriers and the interruption of the radiative recombination process [50]. Specifically, Cu2+ can react with Si and quench its fluorescence. In the presence of Cu2, both deposition-induced quenching and oxidative quenching take place simultaneously [56]. Besides, the lifetime test also supports the explanation because the lifetime of the Cu2+-quenched Si increases with the oxidation process, which is in accordance with the existing theory. To investigate the quenching mechanism, we performed elemental analysis on the nanocomposite after Cu2+ quenching and found the presence of Cu elements even after washing (Fig. 2 F). These results indicate that the quenching of Si fluorescence by Cu2+ is a deposition-type irreversible quenching, consistent with the findings reported in the literature [48,57].

XPS was further employed to analyze the elemental changes in the material before and after Cu2+ quenching. As shown in Fig. S5A, the C-Si probe exhibited essential elements such as C, Si, and O. However, after reacting with trace amounts of Cu2+, the composite material displayed signals for Cu elements. High-resolution XPS spectra further revealed the Cu 2p peaks located at 930.1 eV and 949.4 eV, corresponding to metallic copper, rather than the satellite peaks of Cu2+ (Fig. S5B). This indicates that the Cu2+ were reduced to elemental copper upon interaction with the probe. As shown in Fig. S5C–E, the Si 2p peak analysis showed a significant change in the ratio of SiO2 to Si after the reaction with Cu2+. The ratio shifted from 2.6:1 to 5.5:1, confirming the oxidation of Si during the reaction process. This oxidation is attributed to the irreversible redox reaction between the electrochemically etched Si surface and Cu2+. Unlike the inner filter effect (IFE) quenching mechanism, the luminescent Si surface obtained through electrochemical etching is reductive and can undergo an irreversible redox reaction with Cu2+, thereby storing information in the ratiometric fluorescent probe. Overall, the XPS analysis provided clear evidence of the elemental changes and the oxidation state of Si, highlighting the unique redox interaction between the Si probe and Cu2+, which is crucial for the functionality of the ratiometric fluorescent sensor.

After demonstrating the efficient fluorescence color change of the composite probe induced by Cu2+, we explored its feasibility for oxalate detection and optimized the experimental conditions. Cu2+ are known to have a high affinity for oxalate (KCu-Ox = 2.55 × 1025, 298 K) [58]. Based on this, when Cu2+ are pre-reacted with oxalate, the quenching ability of the complex on the C-Si probe's fluorescence is significantly reduced. Therefore, in the presence of high concentrations of oxalate, the probe exhibits red fluorescence, which is also a commonly used warning color. Firstly, we confirmed that oxalate itself has negligible effects on the dual-color fluorescent probe, indicating that all signal changes are dependent on Cu2+ (Fig. 3
A). Secondly, as shown in Fig. 3 B, the R/B ratio of the system indeed changed significantly after the addition of oxalate, demonstrating the feasibility of detection. Additionally, we found that the difference in the R/B ratio before and after the addition of oxalate was maximized when the probe material concentration was 2 mg/mL. Therefore, we selected this concentration for subsequent experiments. The pre-reaction between Cu2+ and oxalate requires a certain amount of time, so we optimized the reaction time. The results showed that the reaction could be completed within 4 min, indicating its efficiency (Fig. 3 C). Finally, we optimized the pH of the reaction. The results indicated that a pH of 7 yielded the most significant effect (Fig. 3 D). The reason for this phenomenon may be that acidic and alkaline environments can affect the binding of Cu2+ to oxalate, thereby influencing the detection efficiency.

Subsequently, the analytical performance of the probe for oxalate anion in aqueous solution was evaluated under optimized experimental conditions. As illustrated in Fig. 4
A, a pronounced color change from blue to red was observed for the probe with increasing oxalate concentration. Concurrently, the ratiometric fluorescence spectral changes of the probe exhibited a regular variation with increasing oxalate concentration (0–10 mM) (Fig. 4 B). Notably, even at a low concentration of 0.1 μM, a significant change in the fluorescence spectra was detected (Fig. 4 C). The limit of detection (LOD) was determined using the standard deviation of blank measurements (σ) and the slope (k) of the calibration curve according to the IUPAC-recommended formula: LOD = 3σ/k [59]. To obtain σ, we first performed blank measurements by recording the fluorescence response (R/B ratio) of the probe solution without oxalate (n = 10 replicates). After that, the σ was calculated as the standard deviation of these 10 blank measurements. This procedure inherently accounts for instrument noise (baseline fluctuations) as all measurements were conducted under identical experimental conditions (same buffer, probe concentration, and excitation wavelength). Then, as shown in Fig. 4 D, a calibration curve for oxalate detection was further obtained, yielding a satisfactory linear relationship within the range of 10−6–10−2 M. The slope (k = 2.152) was derived from linear regression analysis of the calibration curve and the LOD was calculated to be 0.09 μM. Table S1 compares the analytical performance of this platform with other reported oxalate detection methods, which indicates that the proposed method exhibits excellent analytical performance with the lowest detection limit.

The selectivity of the proposed sensor was further investigated. As shown in Fig. 4 E, common inorganic salts and small molecules found in urine, such as sodium salts, potassium salts, phosphates, glucose, and uric acid, were selected for testing [27]. These interferences were tested at a concentration tenfold higher than that of oxalate (10 mM). The exceptional selectivity arises from the unique binding affinity between oxalate and Cu2+. As reported, oxalate exhibits an extraordinarily high binding constant with Cu2+ in aqueous solution (K = 2.55 × 1025 at 298 K), significantly surpassing that of other common anions (e.g., phosphate, halides) that may also complex with copper ions [58,60]. Similar to previous work, other common interfering species (e.g., cations, glucose, urea) also showed negligible effects on the sensing signal [61]. Therefore, the fluorescence response of the system was scarcely affected by the presence of these interfering substances. These results demonstrate the strong anti-interference capability of the proposed sensor, suggesting its potential for widespread application in real samples.

To validate the reliability of the proposed method, oxalate analysis was performed on six urine samples, and spike recovery tests were conducted using one sample. As summarized in Table S2, the obtained recovery rates ranged from 98.7 % to 106.8 %, thereby confirming the practicality of this method for oxalate analysis in real urine samples. It should be noted that theratiometric (R/B) approachinherently corrects for common interferences by self-referencing the blue fluorescence of CDs (internal standard) against the oxalate-sensitive red fluorescence of Si. This dual-signal normalization minimizes the need for additional corrections for real sample analysis.

Furthermore, urine samples from three patients with urolithiasis and three healthy individuals were simultaneously analyzed using both the standard enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) and the proposed method. As shown in Fig. 5 , the results indicated that the oxalate concentrations in the urine samples from the three healthy individuals (samples 1–3) were below 460 μM (the clinical threshold concentration), whereas the oxalate concentrations in the urine samples from the three patients with urolithiasis (samples 4–6) exceeded 460 μM. The test results obtained using our method were comparable to those obtained using the commercial ELISA method, with no significant differences observed. These findings strongly demonstrate the practical applicability of the novel ratiometric fluorescent sensor for analyzing oxalate levels in the urine of patients with urolithiasis.

In summary, we have developed a dual-color fluorescent C-Si ratiometric fluorescent probe for the rapid and sensitive analysis of oxalate in urine. Specifically, due to the FRET effect, the red-fluorescent mesoporous Si particles can effectively quench the blue-fluorescent CDs. In the presence of trace amounts of Cu2+, the red fluorescence of the C-Si composite probe is quenched by the irreversible deposition and oxidation quenching effect, thereby revealing the blue fluorescence of the CDs. Utilizing the CDs as an internal reference, the ratiometric fluorescent probe exhibits a regular signal change. Leveraging the high affinity of oxalate for copper ions, the presence of oxalate weakens the Cu2+-induced color change of the probe, thus enabling rapid and sensitive detection of oxalate. Under optimized conditions, we have demonstrated that the proposed sensing strategy exhibits excellent detection performance, including high sensitivity and strong anti-interference capability. Moreover, the results from real urine sample analysis have confirmed the accuracy and practical application potential of the ratiometric fluorescent probe. In future work, given the significant advantages of this platform in rapidly and accurately determining oxalate, we will further expand the clinical sample cohort and compare it with clinical outcomes, with the aim of developing it into a valuable point-of-care testing strategy for self-screening of oxalate in the urine of urolithiasis patients.

Qin Weng: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Lidong Cao: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Data curation. Artyom Nikolaevich: Methodology, Investigation. Mengmeng Dong: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Qiuping Sheng: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Zhejiang Province Medical and Health Technology Project (No. 2023562735).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2025.114040.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
